title: Day 03 Interview Prep
description: Practice explaining keys, list rendering, composition, and why reusable components matter in design systems and product teams.

questions.question: Why do we need keys in React lists?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Reconciliation basics — not just 'because React says so'.
keyPoints: Efficient DOM updates when items move
Preserves state in list items correctly
questions.sampleAnswer: Keys tell React which item in a list corresponds to which previous item. When the list changes, React uses keys to diff efficiently and avoid reusing the wrong DOM node. I use stable ids from data — not random values or index when the list can reorder.
followUps: When is index as key acceptable?

questions.question: What is the children prop and how do you use it?
keyPoints: Can be elements, text, or render props later
questions.sampleAnswer: children is the content nested inside a component's JSX tags. A Card component can render {children} in its body so callers pass arbitrary content. I type it React.ReactNode in TypeScript. It is how I build flexible layout wrappers without prop drilling every possible slot.

questions.question: Why build reusable components instead of copying JSX?
questions.sampleAnswer: Reusable components encode design and behavior once. When we fix an accessibility bug in TextInput, every form benefits. Product teams ship faster because engineers compose primitives instead of rewriting markup. The trade-off is upfront API design — props must stay flexible but not bloated.
followUps: How do you version a design system?

questions.question: Explain map, filter, and reduce — when would you use each in a React form?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Array method fluency tied to UI tasks.
keyPoints: map transforms to JSX or new arrays
Often chained in data prep before render
questions.sampleAnswer: map renders a list of fields or options to components. filter removes empty error messages before showing a summary. reduce might count how many fields failed validation. I keep heavy logic outside JSX when readability suffers — prepare data, then map in return.

questions.question: How do you design props for a reusable TextInput?
questions.sampleAnswer: I expose value, onChange, label, error, name, and id. The parent owns state. I extend React.InputHTMLAttributes for optional native props but omit value/onChange from spread to avoid conflicts. Error is optional string; component renders nothing when absent. Keeps the input presentational.

questions.question: What is composition in React?
keyPoints: Build complex UI from simple pieces
questions.sampleAnswer: Composition means assembling UI from smaller components — Button inside Form, FormField wrapping TextInput. React favors composition over inheritance. I might have a generic Box with children and a specialized LoginForm that composes field components without extending a base class.
followUps: How does this relate to slots in Vue?

questions.question: A list of editable rows loses focus when typing after a parent re-render. Keys use array index. What is happening?
keyPoints: Index keys shift when items insert/delete
Focus tied to DOM node identity
Fix: stable id from server or client uuid
questions.sampleAnswer: Index keys cause React to associate row state with position, not identity. When the list reorders or prepends items, indices shift and React reconciles incorrectly — inputs remount and focus jumps. I switch to stable row ids from data. I only use index for static lists that never reorder.
followUps: How generate ids for new rows?

questions.question: Your team has twelve slightly different input components. How do you consolidate without a six-month design system project?
keyPoints: Audit differences — props vs true variants
questions.sampleAnswer: I inventory props each variant needs — often 80% overlap. I extract one TextInput with size and error variants, migrate login and signup first, leave edge cases on old components temporarily. I ship weekly increments with visual regression checks. Full design system is the goal; consolidation is the first PR, not a freeze.
